era_context: The Peloponnesian War continues in its sixth year (431–404 BCE). Athens and Sparta remain locked in a grinding conflict with no clear end in sight. Plague has recently devastated Athens (430–426 BCE), killing a significant portion of the population including Pericles. The war has disrupted agriculture, trade, and normal civic life across Greece. The Persian Empire remains a major power in the eastern Mediterranean. In Italy, Rome is consolidating control over Latium and beginning to expand its influence.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond Greek and Roman awareness
East Asia (contemporary Chinese records exist but are not well integrated into Western historical synthesis)

events.title: End of the Athenian plague
events.description: The devastating plague that struck Athens in 430 BCE finally subsides in 426 BCE. The epidemic has killed an estimated 25–30% of the Athenian population, including the statesman Pericles (429 BCE). The plague's end marks a turning point in Athenian morale and capacity for military action, though the city remains weakened. The psychological and social impact of the plague—including loss of faith in traditional religion and civic institutions—reshapes Athenian culture.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book II–III

events.certainty_note: Thucydides provides a detailed account of the plague's duration and impact. The exact end date is inferred from his narrative; modern scholars generally accept 426 BCE as the approximate end, though some place it in 425 BCE.

events.title: Athenian military campaigns in the Aegean
events.description: With the plague subsiding, Athens resumes more aggressive military operations. Naval expeditions target Spartan-allied cities and secure grain supplies from the Black Sea region. These campaigns are essential to Athenian survival and the maintenance of its maritime empire.
key_figures: Athenian naval commanders (names not consistently recorded for this specific year)

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book III

events.certainty_note: Thucydides documents ongoing naval operations; specific engagements in 426 BCE are not always individually dated.

events.title: Spartan and Corinthian operations in central Greece
events.description: Sparta and its allies continue campaigns to weaken Athenian power. The war remains a stalemate with neither side able to achieve decisive victory. Boeotian forces and Corinthian contingents remain active in support of Spartan strategy.
key_figures: Spartan ephors and generals (names not consistently recorded)

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book III

events.certainty_note: Thucydides documents the general pattern of Spartan operations; specific events in 426 BCE are not always individually dated.

events.title: Athenian expedition to Sicily (preliminary reconnaissance)
events.description: Athens sends preliminary naval expeditions to Sicily to assess the situation and gather intelligence for potential future intervention. These early probes lay the groundwork for the much larger Sicilian Expedition that will be formally launched in 415 BCE. The expeditions are motivated by Athenian ambitions to expand power in the western Mediterranean and support allies like Segesta.
key_figures: Athenian naval commanders (names not consistently recorded for this specific year)

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book III–IV

events.certainty_note: Thucydides mentions Athenian interest in Sicily during this period, but the exact dating of preliminary expeditions is uncertain. Some scholars place these earlier or later.
cross_references: 415 BCE: Major Sicilian Expedition formally launched

disconfirming_evidence: The exact end date of the Athenian plague is debated among scholars. Some place it in 425 BCE rather than 426 BCE. Thucydides' account of the plague is detailed but may contain some exaggerations or inaccuracies regarding mortality rates and duration. The dating of preliminary Athenian expeditions to Sicily is uncertain; some scholars argue these occurred earlier or later than 426 BCE. Modern epidemiological analysis of the plague suggests it may have been typhus, typhoid fever, or another disease rather than bubonic plague, though the exact pathogen remains disputed.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 426 BCE is heavily dependent on Thucydides, who provides a detailed account of the plague and its aftermath. His narrative is generally reliable but reflects Athenian perspectives. Archaeological evidence (inscriptions, coins, pottery) provides supplementary information but rarely allows precise annual dating. The record is biased toward military and political events involving major Greek city-states; events in peripheral regions and among non-elite populations are underrepresented. The plague's impact on non-Athenian populations is poorly documented.

graph_edges.note: End of plague enables resumption of aggressive Athenian military operations
Preliminary Sicilian expeditions in 426 BCE contribute to planning for the major Sicilian Expedition in 415 BCE
Athenian and Spartan military operations occur simultaneously as part of the ongoing Peloponnesian War
